name: Caipora
description: The Caipora is a mischievous creature from Brazilian folklore, often depicted as a small, hairy humanoid figure, sometimes male and sometimes female. They are considered protectors of the forest and its animals. Caiporas are known for playing tricks on hunters, misdirecting them, and scaring away game. They are skilled riders, often depicted riding a collared peccary or other forest animal. Offerings of tobacco are sometimes left to appease them. The Caipora's intentions are not always malicious; they may simply be testing a hunter's respect for nature. They are a reminder to treat the forest and its inhabitants with reverence and to avoid unnecessary hunting. The appearance and behavior of the Caipora vary regionally, but the core characteristics of forest guardian and trickster remain consistent across the diverse landscapes of Brazil. They are deeply ingrained in the cultural fabric of rural communities and serve as a cautionary tale about the consequences of disrespecting the natural world. The stories of the Caipora have been passed down through generations, ensuring their place in Brazilian mythology.
subtitle: Guardian of the Brazilian Forests
appearance: The Caipora is typically described as a small, human-like being covered in thick, dark hair. Their height is often compared to that of a child. They may have large feet turned backwards to confuse trackers. Some depictions show them with a wild, unkempt appearance, while others portray them as more refined. They are often depicted riding a collared peccary or another forest animal. Their eyes are said to glow in the dark.
habitat: Deep within Brazilian forests, particularly in remote and undisturbed areas. They are most commonly associated with the Amazon rainforest and other tropical rainforests.
behavior: The Caipora is known for their mischievous and unpredictable behavior. They enjoy playing tricks on hunters, often misdirecting them or scaring away game. They are fiercely protective of the forest and its animals and will punish those who disrespect them. They are said to be able to communicate with animals and control their movements. They can be appeased with offerings of tobacco.

keyMyths.title: The Misdirected Hunter
keyMyths.description: A hunter disrespects the forest and kills more animals than he needs. The Caipora leads him on a wild goose chase, leaving him lost and without game, teaching him a lesson about respecting nature.

keyMyths.title: The Tobacco Offering
keyMyths.description: A hunter leaves an offering of tobacco for the Caipora before hunting. The Caipora, pleased with the gesture of respect, guides the hunter to a successful hunt, ensuring he only takes what he needs.
